---
#title: "Chapter 4: Non-linear mixed effects models"
#author: "Dagan Lonsdale"
#bibliography: chapter4.bib
#csl: aa.csl

#output:
#  bookdown::html_document2:
#    fig_caption : TRUE
#    number_sections: no
#    toc: yes
#    toc_float: yes
#    latex_engine: xelatex
#    highlight: tango
#    extra_dependencies: "subfig"
#header-includes:
#  - \usepackage{float}
#  - \floatplacement{table}{H}
#  - \usepackage{booktabs}
#  - \usepackage{caption}
#  - \captionsetup{labelfont=bf,margin=12pt}
#urlcolor: blue

title: "Introduction to compartmental analysis"
output:
  bookdown::html_document2:
    fig_caption : TRUE
    number_sections: no
    toc: yes
    toc_float: yes

header-includes:
  - \usepackage{colortbl}
  - \usepackage{multirow}
  - \usepackage{float}
  - \floatplacement{figure}{H}
  - \floatplacement{table}{H}
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \captionsetup{labelfont=bf,margin=12pt} 
  - \newcommand{\blandscape}{\begin{landscape}}
  - \newcommand{\elandscape}{\end{landscape}}

---

<style>
h1.title {
  font-size: 48px;

  text-align: center;
}
h1 {
  font-size: 24px;
}
h2 {
  font-size: 20px;
}
h3 {
  font-size: 16px;
}

h4.author { 
  text-align: center;
}
body {
text-align: justify}
</style>

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, fig.pos= "H")
library(tidyverse)
library(kableExtra)
library(gridExtra)
library(PKNCA) 
library(RColorBrewer)
library(broom)
library(nlmixr2)
library(nlmixr2data)
```

# Learning outcomes  

* List advantages and disadvantages of compartmental analysis
* Describe the difference between a fixed effect and a random effect in a population pharmacokinetic model
* Interpret the key parameter estimates from a fitted model (clearance and volume of distribution)

# Tasks

* Fit a one-compartment IV bolus model for the 'Healthy' dataset
* Fit a one-compartment IV bolus model for the 'Sepsis' dataset
* Compare volume of distribution and clearance values for the two datasets
* Compare <TT>Cl</TT> values using non-compartmental and compartmental analysis

### Extension tasks

* Fit two-compartment models to the teaching and task datasets

# Packages you need for this session

```{r, eval=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(nlmixr2)
```

# Datasets for tasks during this session

```{r datasets, eval=FALSE}

healthy2 <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/c-swire/pkcourse/refs/heads/main/docs/healthy_ca.csv")
sepsis2 <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/c-swire/pkcourse/refs/heads/main/docs/sepsis_ca.csv")

```

When you expect these datasets, you will notice there are several new columns. Many of these are required for non-linear mixed effects modelling and are explained below. 

In addition:

* WT = weight
* CrCL = creatinine clearance
* ALB = albumin

# Teaching data for this session

In this Session the exemplar fits a one-compartment ORAL absorption model to sparse data after a dose of furosemide is taken.

```{r dataset,warning=F,message=F}
sparse<-
 read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dlonsdal/SGUL_PK_data/main/sparsefru.csv")
```

_Hint: The tasks in this session are after IV administration of a drug, so you will need to adapt the model in the example to remove parts relating to absorption_

# Introduction 

'Rich' data are where there are lots of samples for each individual at each time point. This kind of data is really easy to produce in early-phase trials, including entry-into-human studies. 

However, at later phases of drug development, including post-marketing, pharmacokinetic data are often more challenging to obtain. In addition, rigid sampling times are less easy to implement outside of the confines of a dedicated pharmacokinetic study as patients or study participants are often at clinic or study visits at irregular times and so sampling may be somewhat opportunistic. Self-administered doses are also less likely to be accurately taken at prescribed times. These factors make modelling data using the non-compartmental methods we have used so far challenging and less accurate.

The purpose of this session, is to introduce the concept of non-linear mixed-effects modelling. This will include introduction to the modelling package <TT>nlmixr</TT>. This package is challenging to install on your own machine, <TT>install.packages('nlmixr2')</TT> may work. If you run a Windows operating system, you are likely to also need <TT>rtools</TT>. You will need to follow instructions online on how to install and download this. Installation on an Apple Mac usually requires <TT>Xcode command line tools</TT> to be downloaded. We recommend using [Posit Cloud](https://posit.cloud/) for this session. 

## Advantages and disadvantages of non-linear mixed-effects models

**Advantages of non-linear mixed-effects approach**

* Enables modelling of sparse data, useful in later phase/post-marketing studies
* Allows more complex identification of covariate effects (for example organ function)
* Alternative sampling and modelling may allow for populations to be studied that are not straightforward to study in traditional phase-0/1 design (for example children, or drugs in pregnancy)
* Allows complex simulations which can inform subsequent study design and drug dosing

**Disadvantages of non-linear mixed-effects approach**

* Feels inherently more complex which can make people anxious about *undertaking* or *understanding* the process
* Computationally expensive
* Time consuming
* Often requires time cleaning/organising data from multiple sources 

# What is the point of a model?

It is clear that understanding data from a clinical study is important. Pharmacokinetic data are the same as any other output from a study. It is important to understand the data, what they mean for the absorption, distribution, metabolism and excretion (ADME) of the compound. They allow us to make suggestions for the next stage of drug development or to optimise dosing for different populations in clinical practice. This might include dose selection, frequency of administration or even decisions on formulation.

Compartmental pharmacokinetic models simplify complex human physiology by describing the concentration-time course of a drug with (relatively) simple mathematical equations. These mathematical equations help to quantify ADME processes through the estimation of values for parameters such as clearance and volume of distribution.

## One-compartment IV bolus model

In the one compartment model IV bolus model, the body is simplified and represented as a single box, into which the dose of the drug is deposited instantaneously. Drug is assumed to distribute evenly and instantaneously throughout this compartment such that the concentration is assumed to be the same wherever you are within it.


```{r onecpt, echo = FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='One-compartment model',fig.height=2,out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("onecpt1.png")
```


This single compartment is assumed to receive the drug instantly at the time of the intravenous dose. The compartment has a volume $V_1$, which in a one-compartment model is equal to the volume of distribution ($V_d$) of the drug. The concentration in this compartment is equal to the plasma concentration ($C_p$) and the amount of drug in the compartment ($A_1$) is equal to the total amount of drug in the body ($A_b$). Elimination of the drug is usually assumed to be a first-order process with elimination rate constant $k_e$. 

## Relationship between $k_e$, $V_d$ and $Cl$ in the IV bolus model

$k_e, V_1$ and clearance ($Cl$) are related \begin{equation} k_e=\frac{Cl}{V_1} \end{equation}.  

## Concentration-time equation for the one-compartment IV bolus model

The concentration $C$ at time $t$ for the one-compartment IV bolus model is \begin{equation} C=C_0e^{-kt} \end{equation} or, if you prefer to use clearance and volume of distribution \begin{equation} C=C_0e^{-\frac{Cl}{Vd}t} \end{equation} $C_0=$ Drug concentration at time $t=0$. 

# Population pharmacokinetic modelling

## What is mixed effects modelling?

In a population pharmacokinetic model, we assume that each individual's pharmacokinetic parameters (e.g. clearance) are made up of two components.

The fixed effect represents the typical value across the population. For example, the typical clearance of Drug A in healthy volunteers. This is what we usually report as the headline result of a population pharmacokinetic analysis.

The random effect represents how much any given individual deviates from that typical value. Not everyone has the same clearance. Some patients clear the drug faster, some slower. The random effect captures this between-subject variability. We cannot predict it perfectly, but we can estimate how large it is.

A mixed-effects model contains both components. 

### How this applies to compartmental pharmacokinetic modelling

Let's return to the one-compartment IV-bolus model once more. Let us parameterise using *primary* pharmacokinetic parameters (clearance and volume of distribution). Broadly, our model remains the same $$C=C_{0}e^{-\frac{Cl}{V}t}$$ however, we now add a further layer, by stating that our parameters are made up of a fixed (population mean, $\theta$) effect and a random (inter-individual variability, $\eta$) effect. $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_{Cl}}$$ the modelling process then estimates $$\theta_{Cl}\textrm{ -the population mean value for clearance (a fixed effect)}$$ and makes some estimate of $$\eta_{Cl} \textrm{ -individual variability of clearance (a random-effect)}$$

Similarly, we model volume of distribution as $$V=\theta_{V}\times e^{\eta_{V}}$$ 

# Non-linear mixed-effects modelling in <TT>R</TT>  

Non-linear mixed-effects modelling is computationally complex and requires knowledge of statistics, pharmacological models and how to use computer code. We are going to enter into the world of population pharmacokinetic modelling by using the package <TT>nlmixr</TT>. 

## Using <TT>nlmixr</TT>

First you need to call <TT>library(nlmixr2)</TT> to load the package. 

In this example we fit a one-compartment oral-absorption model to sparse data after an oral dose of furosemide is taken. 

```{r fru2,warning=F,message=F}
sparse<-
 read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dlonsdal/SGUL_PK_data/main/sparsefru.csv")
```

```{r fruhead,warning=F,message=F, include=FALSE}
kbl(head(sparse), booktabs = TRUE, caption = "First few rows of the furosemide dataset") %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("striped"))

```

<TT>nlmixr2</TT> requires data to be structured and labelled in a particular way. 

* ID = patient ID
* TIME = time (since administration of dose)
* DV = dependent variable (drug concentration)
* AMT = amount (dose)
* EVID = event identifier (tells <TT>nlmixr2</TT> whether that row represents an observation or dose event)
* CMT = compartment 

Then you need to build your model. When building the model, you specify two blocks in a standard <TT>R function()</TT>. 

### Ini block

```{r, eval = F}

ini({ 
    tka <-log(1.3) 
    tcl <- log(8) 
    tv <- log(20)
    eta.ka~0.1 
    eta.cl ~ 0.1
    eta.v ~ 0.1
    add.sd <- 0.1 
  })
```

The first part of the function is to set the initial estimates for your model parameters, this is known as the <TT>ini()</TT> block. We are parameterising using absorption rate constant ($k_a$), clearance ($Cl$) and volume of distribution ($V$) in a one-compartment oral-absorption model. So we need to tell <TT>nlmixr</TT> initial values for each of these parameters *as well as* values for the random effects associated with each parameter. 

As described above, the population mean of fixed effects are termed _theta_. In the <TT>ini()</TT> block, <TT>tcl</TT> represents the log of typical clearance. <TT>exp(tcl)</TT> therefore gives you ${\theta}_{Cl}$, the typical population <TT>Cl</TT>. 

The <TT>eta</TT> terms in the code below are initial estimates of the variance of the inter-individual variability _eta_. 

A further source of variability captured by the model is residual variability, which captures everything that remains unexplained after accounting for inter-individual variability.
This includes random fluctuations in drug concentrations, errors in recording the exact time a sample was taken or a dose was administered, and assay measurement error. Residual variability is not attributed to any specific cause. It is the irreducible noise around the model's predictions. In the code, this is represented by <TT>add.sd</TT> (additive residual error). 

### Additive versus proportional residual error

In `nlmixr2`, you can specify these as:

```{r errortypes, eval=FALSE}
# Additive error
linCmt() ~ add(add.sd)

# Proportional error
linCmt() ~ prop(prop.sd)

# Combined additive and proportional (most flexible)
linCmt() ~ add(add.sd) + prop(prop.sd)
```

An **additive** residual error model assumes the size of the error is constant regardless of the magnitude of the concentration. This can work well when concentrations are measured over a narrow range.

However, analytical assays typically have a **proportional** error — the absolute error is larger at higher concentrations. A proportional error model accounts for this:

I have used an **additive** residual error model in all the examples, but you can try running the models with other residual error models as above to see whether this changes the model diagnostics (which we will cover in session 3).

### Choosing initial estimates

If your initial estimates are too far from the true values, the fitting algorithm may fail to converge, or may converge to an incorrect solution.

A useful starting point is to use your NCA results from Session 1. 
For inter-individual variability ($\eta$), starting values are typically 0.1. 
Starting values for residual error of around 0.1–0.2 are generally reasonable.

### Model block

```{r, eval = F}

model({ 
    ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka)
    cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)
    v <- exp(tv + eta.v)
    linCmt() ~ add(add.sd) 
  })
```

The `model()` block defines how the pharmacokinetic parameters are calculated for each individual. Each line transforms the population-level fixed effects ($\theta$, represented by `tcl`, `tv` and `tka`) and individual random effects ($\eta$) into individual parameter estimates using an exponential function. For example:

$$Cl_i = e^{\theta_{Cl} + \eta_{Cl}}$$

The exponential transformation is used for two reasons. First, it ensures that parameter estimates are always positive — a negative clearance or volume of distribution is physiologically impossible. Second, it places the inter-individual variability on a log-normal scale, which is consistent with the way pharmacokinetic parameters are typically distributed in a population.

`linCmt()` solves the compartmental model equations based on the parameters defined above. The `~ add(add.sd)` part specifies the residual error model.

After building the model we can run a check using <TT>nlmixr(one.cmt)</TT>, which describes the model we have built. And then we fit the model to the data.

```{r nlmixr,warning=F,message=F, results='hide'}

# First make the model by combining the ini and model blocks
one.cmt <- function() {
  ini({ 
    tka <-log(1.3) # set the initial estimates for each parameter, note that ka will not be required for IV datasets
    tcl <- log(8) 
    tv <- log(20)
    eta.ka~0.1 # inter-individual variabilities, again note ka line this is not required for IV datasets
    eta.cl ~ 0.1
    eta.v ~ 0.1
    add.sd <- 0.1 # residual variability
  })
  model({ # define the model
    ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka) # ka will not be required for IV datasets
    cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)
    v <- exp(tv + eta.v)
    linCmt() ~ add(add.sd) 
  })
}

# Fit the data using this model
fit<-nlmixr2(one.cmt, sparse, est="saem", list(print=0)) # recall that we have named the dataset 'sparse'

```

You can run <TT>print(fit)</TT> and you will get an enormous amount of output from your model. Tables  \@ref(tab:nlmixfix) & \@ref(tab:nlmixran) show the fixed and random parameter estimates, along with confidence intervals. Don't worry about the other output for now.
```{r nlmixfix,echo=F,warning=T,message=F}

kbl(fit$par.fixed %>% 
      select(-last_col(),-6),booktabs = T, caption="Fixed effect parameter estimates from Pop-PK analysis of sparse furosemide data")%>%  kable_styling(latex_options =c("striped"))


```

```{r nlmixran,echo=F,warning=T,message=F}
kbl(signif(diag(fit$omegaR),2), caption="Random effects parameter estimates from Pop-PK analysis of sparse furosemide data",booktabs = T,col.names = c(
                           "Estimate"))%>%  kable_styling(latex_options =c("striped"))
```
